entities.name: ESSENCE

attributes.primary_endpoint: resolution of steatohepatitis without worsening of liver fibrosis; reduction in liver fibrosis without worsening of steatohepatitis
attributes.patient_population: adults with biopsy-defined MASH and fibrosis stage 2 or 3

entities.context: In this ongoing phase 3, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, we assigned 1197 patients with biopsy-defined MASH and fibrosis stage 2 or 3 in a 2:1 ratio to receive once-weekly subcutaneous semaglutide at a dose of 2.4 mg or placebo for 240 weeks. (Funded by Novo Nordisk; ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT04822181.)

entities.name: Gastrointestinal Adverse Events
entities.context: Gastrointestinal adverse events were more common in the semaglutide group.

entities.name: liver histologic improvement

attributes.clinical_relevance: primary endpoint for MASH treatment response

entities.context: In patients with MASH and moderate or advanced liver fibrosis, once-weekly semaglutide at a dose of 2.4 mg improved liver histologic results.

relations.evidence: Semaglutide, a glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist, is a candidate for the treatment of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH).
Semaglutide, a glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist, is a candidate for the treatment of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH).
In patients with MASH and moderate or advanced liver fibrosis, once-weekly semaglutide at a dose of 2.4 mg improved liver histologic results.
we assigned 1197 patients with biopsy-defined MASH and fibrosis stage 2 or 3 in a 2:1 ratio to receive once-weekly subcutaneous semaglutide at a dose of 2.4 mg or placebo for 240 weeks.
Gastrointestinal adverse events were more common in the semaglutide group.
(Funded by Novo Nordisk; ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT04822181.)
(Funded by Novo Nordisk; ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT04822181.)
Title: Phase 3 Trial of Semaglutide in Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatohepatitis. Journal: The New England journal of medicine (2025). PMID: 40305708.

Resolution of steatohepatitis without worsening of fibrosis occurred in 62.9% of the 534 patients in the semaglutide group and in 34.3% of the 266 patients in the placebo group (estimated difference, 28.7 percentage points; 95% confidence interval [CI], 21.1 to 36.2; P<0.001).

A reduction in liver fibrosis without worsening of steatohepatitis was reported in 36.8% of the patients in the semaglutide group and in 22.4% of those in the placebo group (estimated difference, 14.4 percentage points; 95% CI, 7.5 to 21.3; P<0.001).
In patients with MASH and moderate or advanced liver fibrosis, once-weekly semaglutide at a dose of 2.4 mg improved liver histologic results.

A reduction in liver fibrosis without worsening of steatohepatitis was reported in 36.8% of the patients in the semaglutide group and in 22.4% of those in the placebo group (estimated difference, 14.4 percentage points; 95% CI, 7.5 to 21.3; P<0.001).
The mean change in body weight was -10.5% with semaglutide and -2.0% with placebo (estimated difference, -8.5 percentage points; 95% CI, -9.6 to -7.4; P<0.001).
In patients with MASH and moderate or advanced liver fibrosis, once-weekly semaglutide at a dose of 2.4 mg improved liver histologic results.
